summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the component of weight parallel to an incline?

Inclined Planes Decompose weight into two components: parallel (mg sin θ, down the slope) and perpendicular (mg cos θ, into the surface). The other options — 'mg cos θ', 'mg tan θ', and 'mg/sin θ' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is the normal force on an inclined plane?

The normal force is the contact force perpendicular to a surface. On a flat surface: N = mg. On an incline: N = mg·cos(θ). It adjusts to prevent objects from passing through surfaces. The answer is 'N = mg cos θ'.

On a frictionless incline, what is the acceleration?

On a frictionless incline, only the parallel component of gravity accelerates the object down the slope. The acceleration is a = g sin θ. At θ = 0° (flat), a = 0. At θ = 90° (vertical), a = g.

What is the angle of repose?

Key Angles The angle of repose is the steepest angle at which an object stays stationary: tan θ = μₛ. The other options — 'The angle of a ramp in a lab', '45 degrees', and 'The angle at which friction disappears' — are related concepts but do not correctly answer this specific question.

What is the normal force on a flat horizontal surface for a 5 kg object? (Use g = 10 m/s²)

On a flat horizontal surface, the normal force equals the weight: N = mg = 5 kg × 10 m/s² = 50 N. The surface pushes up with exactly enough force to support the object's weight.

What is the definition of 'normal force'?

The normal force is the contact force that a surface exerts perpendicular to itself on any object resting on or pressing against it. The word 'normal' in physics means 'perpendicular.' It prevents objects from passing through solid surfaces.

A student places a 4 kg block on a frictionless ramp tilted at 30°. What is the acceleration of the block down the ramp? (Use g = 10 m/s², sin 30° = 0.5)

On a frictionless incline, a = g sin θ = 10 × sin 30° = 10 × 0.5 = 5 m/s². The mass cancels out — all objects on a frictionless incline at the same angle have the same acceleration, regardless of mass.

A student measures the normal force on a 10 kg block sitting on a ramp tilted at 60°. What is the normal force? (Use g = 10 m/s², cos 60° = 0.5)

The normal force on an incline is N = mg cos θ = 10 kg × 10 m/s² × cos 60° = 100 × 0.5 = 50 N. The steeper the angle, the smaller the normal force becomes because less of the weight pushes into the surface.

A student notices that a toy car rolls faster down a steep ramp than a gentle ramp. What is the physics explanation?

The parallel component of gravity is mg sin θ. A steeper ramp has a larger angle θ, which increases sin θ, making the force pulling the car down the slope larger. This produces a greater acceleration. The normal force actually decreases on steeper ramps.
